subtitle: Enalapril in severe HF (NYHA IV)
endpoint: All-cause mortality at 6 months
finding: Enalapril reduced mortality 40% vs placebo (26% vs 44%, p=0.002) in NYHA IV HFrEF. Benefits within 6 months. Neurohormonal hypothesis confirmed.
impact: First mortality trial in HF. ACE inhibitors became cornerstone GDMT. Established that RAAS inhibition reduces mortality, not just symptoms.

subtitle: Enalapril in mild-moderate HFrEF
endpoint: Death or HF hospitalization
finding: Enalapril reduced death/HF hospitalization 26% vs placebo. Mortality reduced 16% (35.2% vs 39.7%). Benefit across full NYHA II-III spectrum.
impact: Expanded ACEi indication beyond NYHA IV. Underpins ACEi Class I recommendation in all HFrEF (EF ≤40%).

subtitle: Metoprolol succinate in HFrEF
endpoint: All-cause mortality
finding: Metoprolol succinate reduced all-cause mortality 34% (7.2% vs 11.0% annualized, p<0.0001). Study terminated early. Only metoprolol SUCCINATE (not tartrate) has mortality benefit.
impact: Along with CIBIS-II and COPERNICUS, established beta-blockers as essential HFrEF GDMT. Only bisoprolol, carvedilol, and metoprolol succinate are evidence-based in HFrEF.

subtitle: Spironolactone in severe HFrEF
endpoint: All-cause mortality
finding: Spironolactone 25mg reduced mortality 30% (35% vs 46%, p<0.001) and HF hospitalizations 35%. Terminated early at 24 months.
impact: Established MRAs as GDMT in HFrEF (EF ≤35%, NYHA III-IV). EMPHASIS-HF (2011) confirmed eplerenone even in NYHA II. Monitor K+ and creatinine.

subtitle: Sacubitril/valsartan vs enalapril in HFrEF
endpoint: CV death or HF hospitalization
finding: ARNI reduced primary endpoint 20% (HR 0.80, p<0.001) vs enalapril. CV death reduced 20%. All-cause mortality reduced 16%. NNT=32 for preventing one death.
impact: ARNI replaced ACEi/ARB as preferred RAAS agent in HFrEF (ACC/AHA 2022 Class I, direct-to-ARNI preferred). Requires 36h washout from ACEi to avoid angioedema. Pearl: start low (24/26mg BID), double every 2-4 weeks to target 97/103mg BID.

subtitle: Dapagliflozin in HFrEF (with/without diabetes)
endpoint: CV death, worsening HF, or urgent HF visit
finding: Dapagliflozin reduced primary endpoint 26% (HR 0.74, p<0.001). Benefit consistent in patients without diabetes (54% of trial). NNT=21 over 18 months.
impact: First SGLT2i trial specifically in HFrEF. Established SGLT2i as 4th GDMT pillar (Class I in 2022 ACC/AHA guidelines, regardless of diabetes). Mechanism: osmotic diuresis, anti-inflammatory, and mitochondrial cardioprotection.

subtitle: Empagliflozin in HFrEF
endpoint: CV death or HF hospitalization
finding: Empagliflozin reduced composite endpoint 25% (HR 0.75, p<0.001). HF hospitalizations reduced 30%. Significant eGFR slope benefit.
impact: Confirmed SGLT2i class effect in HFrEF. Together with DAPA-HF, SGLT2i became the 4th cornerstone of GDMT. Renal protection bonus: slower eGFR decline.

subtitle: Empagliflozin in HFpEF (EF>40%)
endpoint: CV death or HF hospitalization
finding: Empagliflozin reduced composite endpoint 21% (HR 0.79, p<0.001) in HFpEF/HFmrEF. First positive pharmacotherapy trial in HFpEF. Driven mainly by HF hospitalizations.
impact: First drug to show significant benefit in HFpEF. DELIVER (dapagliflozin, 2022) confirmed class effect. SGLT2i now recommended across full HF spectrum — HFrEF, HFmrEF, and HFpEF.

subtitle: Dapagliflozin in HFpEF
endpoint: CV death, worsening HF, or urgent HF visit
finding: Dapagliflozin reduced primary endpoint 18% (HR 0.82, p<0.001) in HFpEF/HFmrEF (EF>40%). Benefit in recently-hospitalized patients. Combined SGLT2i HFpEF meta-analysis NNT ~30.
impact: Confirmed dual SGLT2i class effect in HFpEF. Both empagliflozin and dapagliflozin FDA-approved for HFpEF. Practical pearl: can be initiated in hospital for HF and continued after discharge.

subtitle: Vericiguat in worsening HFrEF
endpoint: CV death or HF hospitalization
finding: Vericiguat reduced primary endpoint 10% vs placebo (HR 0.90, p=0.02) in high-risk worsening HFrEF. Absolute risk reduction 4.2%. NNT=24.
impact: Vericiguat (Verquvo) FDA-approved 2021. Modest benefit — indicated after a worsening HF event on background GDMT. Novel mechanism: stimulates sGC (NO pathway) to improve cardiac/vascular function.

subtitle: Spironolactone in HFpEF
endpoint: CV death, cardiac arrest, HF hospitalization
finding: Primary endpoint NOT met overall (HR 0.89, p=0.14). Americas subgroup showed benefit; Russia/Georgia contamination suspected (low event rates). Significant hyperkalemia 5x higher.
impact: No Class I indication for MRA in HFpEF. However, consider MRA for HFpEF with diuretic need. Eplerenone post-MI HF data (EPHESUS 2003): reduces mortality in HFrEF post-MI. Avoid in CrCl <30 or K+ >5.

subtitle: IV ferric carboxymaltose in HF + iron deficiency
endpoint: HF hospitalization + CV death at 52 weeks
finding: Ferric carboxymaltose reduced HF hospitalizations (−0.30 events/patient-year, p=0.013). CV death not significantly reduced. QoL improved. Iron deficiency: ferritin <100 or ferritin 100-299 with TSAT <20%.
impact: IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose or ferric derisomaltose) recommended Class IIa in HFrEF with iron deficiency to reduce hospitalizations. Oral iron is NOT effective in HF (poor absorption). Screen: ferritin + TSAT in all HF patients.

subtitle: Ticagrelor vs clopidogrel in ACS
endpoint: CV death, MI, stroke at 12 months
finding: Ticagrelor reduced primary endpoint 16% (9.8% vs 11.7%, HR 0.84, p<0.001). CV mortality reduced 21%. All-cause mortality reduced 22%. Non-CABG major bleeding higher with ticagrelor.
impact: Ticagrelor preferred P2Y12 for ACS (NSTEMI/STEMI regardless of management). Critical pearl: avoid aspirin >100mg/day with ticagrelor (PLATO Americas subgroup — higher aspirin doses attenuate ticagrelor benefit). Dyspnea in 14% — typically transient, not bronchospasm.

subtitle: Prasugrel vs clopidogrel in ACS/PCI
endpoint: CV death, MI, stroke at 15 months
finding: Prasugrel reduced primary endpoint 19% (9.9% vs 12.1%, HR 0.81, p<0.001) but significantly more major bleeding (2.4% vs 1.8%). Net harm in prior CVA/TIA, age ≥75, weight <60kg subgroups.
impact: Prasugrel for ACS with PCI (anatomy must be known). Absolute contraindication: prior CVA/TIA. Reduce to 5mg/day if age ≥75 or weight <60kg. Faster, more consistent P2Y12 inhibition than clopidogrel (not affected by CYP2C19 polymorphisms).

subtitle: Low-dose rivaroxaban + aspirin in stable CAD/PAD
endpoint: CV death, stroke, or MI
finding: Rivaroxaban 2.5mg BID + aspirin reduced MACE 24% vs aspirin alone (4.1% vs 5.4%, HR 0.76, p<0.001). CV death reduced 22%. More major bleeding (3.1% vs 1.9%), but no more fatal or intracranial bleeding.
impact: Rivaroxaban 2.5mg BID FDA-approved as adjunct to aspirin for stable CAD/PAD. "Dual pathway inhibition" concept. Net clinical benefit — vascular death reduction outweighs bleeding increase. Not standard for all CAD; use in those without high bleeding risk.

subtitle: Dabigatran vs warfarin in nonvalvular AFib
drug: Dabigatran 150mg BID or 110mg BID (direct thrombin inhibitor)
endpoint: Stroke or systemic embolism
finding: Dabigatran 150mg BID superior to warfarin for stroke/embolism (1.11% vs 1.71%/yr, p<0.001). Dabigatran 110mg BID noninferior with less major bleeding than warfarin. More GI bleeding with 150mg BID.
impact: First DOAC for AFib stroke prevention. Key PK: 80% renally eliminated, half-life 12-17h, no hepatic CYP metabolism. Reduce to 75mg BID if CrCl 15-30. AVOID CrCl <15. Reversal: idarucizumab (Praxbind, 5g IV x2). P-gp interactions (amiodarone, verapamil).

subtitle: Rivaroxaban vs warfarin in AFib
endpoint: Stroke or systemic embolism
finding: Rivaroxaban 20mg daily noninferior to warfarin (1.7% vs 2.2%/yr, HR 0.88, p<0.001 NI). Less ICH and fatal bleeding. More GI bleeding.
impact: Rivaroxaban once-daily advantage for adherence. CRITICAL Pearl: must take 20mg WITH EVENING MEAL (food increases bioavailability from 66% to ~100%). Reduce to 15mg/day if CrCl 15-50. Reversal: andexanet alfa. CYP3A4/P-gp interactions.

subtitle: Apixaban vs warfarin in AFib
endpoint: Stroke or systemic embolism
finding: Apixaban 5mg BID superior to warfarin: stroke/embolism 27% reduction (1.27% vs 1.60%/yr, p<0.001). Also less major bleeding (HR 0.69, p<0.001) AND less mortality (HR 0.89, p=0.047). Triple advantage over warfarin.
impact: Only DOAC superior to warfarin in all 3 key outcomes (efficacy, safety, mortality). Use 2.5mg BID if ≥2 of: age ≥80, weight ≤60kg, SCr ≥1.5. Least renal dependence (27% renal excretion). Reversal: andexanet alfa.

subtitle: AUC/MIC-based vancomycin monitoring consensus
endpoint: Optimal TDM strategy for serious MRSA infections
finding: AUC24/MIC target 400-600 mg·h/L (assuming MIC=1 μg/mL). Trough-only monitoring no longer recommended for serious infections. Bayesian software preferred for AUC estimation. Initial dosing: 15-20 mg/kg IV q8-12h for serious infections.
impact: Practice-changing: troughs 15-20 often result in AUC >600 → nephrotoxicity. AUC-guided dosing reduces nephrotoxicity without sacrificing efficacy. Pearl: AUC 400-600 remains target even if MIC=2 (calculate AUC/MIC). Implement Bayesian software where available.

subtitle: Piperacillin-tazobactam vs meropenem for ESBL bacteremia
endpoint: 30-day mortality for gram-negative bacteremia
finding: Pip-tazo definitive therapy → significantly higher 30-day mortality vs meropenem (12.3% vs 3.7%, RR 3.4, p=0.001). Stopped early for harm.
impact: "MERINO effect" — pip-tazo must NOT be used as definitive therapy for ESBL or cephalosporin-resistant bacteremia even if susceptible in vitro (inoculum effect makes in vitro susceptibility misleading). Meropenem or ertapenem preferred. Exception: ESBL UTI (lower inoculum) may tolerate pip-tazo.

subtitle: Beta-lactam vs FQ vs combination for hospitalized CAP
drug: Amoxicillin vs Moxifloxacin vs Amoxicillin-Clavulanate + Azithromycin
endpoint: 90-day mortality in hospitalized non-ICU CAP
finding: Non-inferiority confirmed: beta-lactam monotherapy ≈ FQ monotherapy ≈ beta-lactam + macrolide for non-severe CAP. 90-day mortality 9% vs 11% vs 9%. No significant differences.
impact: Supports guideline-concordant beta-lactam + macrolide or respiratory FQ for hospitalized non-ICU CAP. Combination (beta-lactam + macrolide) preferred for ICU/severe CAP: macrolide provides immunomodulatory/anti-inflammatory benefit beyond atypical coverage.

subtitle: Balanced crystalloids vs normal saline for resuscitation
endpoint: MAKE30 (death, RRT, or creatinine doubling at 30 days)
finding: Balanced crystalloids reduced MAKE30 vs normal saline in ICU (14.3% vs 15.4%, OR 0.90, p=0.04). Sepsis subgroup had 3.8% absolute benefit. SALT-ED: similar in non-ICU setting.
impact: Balanced crystalloids preferred over NS for large-volume resuscitation. NS causes hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis (Cl− causes afferent arteriolar vasoconstriction → AKI). Exception: avoid LR in severe hypercalcemia, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia. NS preferred for hypovolemic alkalosis.

subtitle: Hydrocortisone in septic shock (RCT)
endpoint: 90-day mortality in vasopressor-dependent septic shock
finding: Hydrocortisone did NOT reduce 90-day mortality (27.9% vs 28.8%, p=0.50). Faster shock resolution (vasopressor-free days improved). No increase in GI bleed or superinfection.
impact: Paired with APROCCHSS (2018, hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone → mortality benefit). Conflicting results. SSC 2021: weak recommendation for corticosteroids in refractory septic shock (not routine). If used: hydrocortisone 200mg/day (continuous infusion or 50mg q6h) + consider fludrocortisone 50mcg/day PO.

subtitle: Hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone in septic shock
endpoint: 90-day mortality
finding: Combination reduced 90-day mortality (43.0% vs 49.1%, p=0.03). Also reduced 28-day mortality and vasopressor days. No excess GI bleeding.
impact: Conflicting results vs ADRENAL. The mineralocorticoid (fludrocortisone) component may be key. SSC 2021: weak recommendation to consider in refractory septic shock. Practical: start when norepinephrine ≥0.25 mcg/kg/min at 4h OR unstable hemodynamics.

subtitle: Dopamine vs norepinephrine as first-line vasopressor
endpoint: 28-day mortality
finding: No overall mortality difference. Dopamine: significantly more arrhythmias (24.1% vs 12.4%, p<0.001). Cardiogenic shock subgroup: dopamine had higher 28-day mortality.
impact: Norepinephrine is first-line vasopressor for septic shock. Dopamine associated with significantly more arrhythmias — avoid in hemodynamically unstable patients. "Renal-dose dopamine" (1-3 mcg/kg/min) does NOT protect kidneys (DOPAMINE trial, BMJ 2000).

subtitle: Vasopressin vs norepinephrine in septic shock
endpoint: Kidney failure-free days at 28 days
finding: No significant difference in kidney failure-free days (57.3% vs 60.1%). No significant reduction in RRT need or mortality. Vasopressin did not reduce AKI as hypothesized.
impact: Vasopressin as NE-sparing adjunct remains reasonable (weak SSC recommendation). Does not save kidneys. Benefits: catecholamine-sparing, possible benefit in patients with arrhythmias from catecholamines. Add vasopressin at NE >0.25-0.5 mcg/kg/min before escalating NE further.

subtitle: Low tidal volume ventilation in ARDS
drug: 6 mL/kg IBW vs 12 mL/kg IBW ventilation strategy
endpoint: In-hospital mortality
finding: Low Vt (6 mL/kg IBW) reduced mortality 22% (31% vs 39.8%, p=0.007). More ventilator-free days. Plateau pressure <30 cmH2O.
impact: Lung-protective ventilation standard of care. Key targets: 6 mL/kg IBW Vt (use IBW not actual weight), plateau pressure <30, driving pressure <15 cmH2O, permissive hypercapnia acceptable (pH >7.20). Escalate PEEP using ARDSNet PEEP-FiO2 table.

subtitle: Neuromuscular blockade in ARDS — conflicting trials
endpoint: Mortality at 90 days
finding: ACURASYS (2010): NMB reduced 90-day adjusted mortality (HR 0.68). ROSE (2019): Early NMB showed NO mortality benefit vs light sedation control (42.5% vs 42.8%). Key difference: ROSE used lighter sedation in controls.
impact: NMB for ARDS is NOT routinely recommended (ROSE). Consider NMB (cisatracurium 37.5 mg/hr) for: severe hypoxia (P/F <150) refractory to optimization, severe ventilator dyssynchrony, or prone positioning facilitation. Duration <48h. Monitor train-of-four.

subtitle: ICU Liberation Bundle — delirium, sedation, mobility
drug: Protocolized sedation + early mobility + family engagement
endpoint: Delirium, coma, mortality, ICU-LOS, return home
finding: Each 10% increase in bundle adherence → 15% lower odds of death (p<0.001), 2% reduction in next-day delirium (p=0.04), 6% improvement in discharge to home (p<0.001).
impact: A=Assess/treat pain (CPOT, NRS). B=SAT + SBT trials daily. C=Choose light sedation (RASS -1 to 0), analgesia-first. D=Delirium monitoring (CAM-ICU or ICDSC). E=Early mobility (PT/OT day 2-3). F=Family engagement/empowerment. Avoid benzodiazepines.

subtitle: Targeted temperature management post-cardiac arrest
endpoint: All-cause mortality at 6 months
finding: NO significant difference in 6-month mortality (50% vs 48%, p=0.37). No difference in neurologic outcomes. More arrhythmias with hypothermia (24% vs 17%). Study used no fever management in control arm.
impact: TTM2 effectively overturned routine cooling to 33°C. Current: PREVENT FEVER (>37.7-38°C) in comatose post-ROSC patients for at least 72h — fever is clearly harmful. Active cooling to 33°C no longer recommended routinely. Maintain normothermia.

subtitle: tPA for ischemic stroke up to 6h, all ages
endpoint: Oxford Handicap Score 0-2 at 6 months
finding: No significant difference in good functional outcome at 6 months (37% vs 35%). More early deaths (11% vs 7%) offset by later functional benefit. Trend to benefit within 4.5h window.
impact: Expanded tPA to older adults (no upper age limit) and 3-4.5h window (ECASS-3). WAKE-UP trial (2018): DWI-FLAIR mismatch on MRI identifies patients with unknown onset (wake-up stroke) who benefit from tPA. Current: alteplase up to 4.5h, tenecteplase emerging as simpler alternative.

subtitle: Mechanical thrombectomy — extended 6-24h window
design: Both RCT, stopped early for benefit
endpoint: Functional independence at 90 days (mRS 0-2)
finding: DAWN: 49% vs 13% functional independence (p<0.001). DEFUSE-3: 45% vs 17% (OR 2.77, p<0.001). Both required imaging-based patient selection (clinical-core mismatch / perfusion mismatch).
impact: Extended mechanical thrombectomy window to 24h for SELECTED patients with salvageable penumbra. Requires RAPID (or equivalent) imaging. Modern thrombectomy for large vessel occlusion reduces disability NNT ~3. Pearl: patient selection by imaging, NOT time alone.

subtitle: Intensive BP control in intracerebral hemorrhage
endpoint: Death or major disability (mRS 3-6) at 90 days
finding: Primary endpoint not significantly different. mRS shift analysis showed benefit with intensive control (OR 0.87, p=0.04). ATACH-2 (2016): target <120 showed NO benefit and more AKI.
impact: AHA/ASA guideline: SBP <140 reasonable in ICH with presenting SBP 150-220 (Class IIa). DO NOT target <120 (ATACH-2 harm). Start IV antihypertensive (nicardipine, labetalol, or clevidipine) promptly. Avoid sodium nitroprusside (ICP effect).

subtitle: Dual antiplatelet (clopidogrel + aspirin) for minor stroke/TIA
drug: Clopidogrel 300mg load then 75mg + Aspirin 50-325mg x21d vs aspirin alone
endpoint: Major ischemic event at 90 days
finding: DAPT reduced major ischemic events 25% (5.0% vs 6.5%, HR 0.75, p=0.02). More major hemorrhage (0.9% vs 0.4%). Benefit concentrated in first 7 days of event.
impact: DAPT (clopidogrel loading 300mg, then 75mg + aspirin) recommended for 21 days after high-risk TIA or minor stroke (NIHSS ≤3). Then aspirin alone. CHANCE trial (2013, China) confirmed at 90 days. Avoid DAPT >90 days (excess bleeding without added efficacy per CHARISMA trial).

subtitle: Intensive glycemic control in newly diagnosed T2DM
endpoint: Composite microvascular and macrovascular complications
finding: Intensive control (HbA1c 7.0% vs 7.9%) reduced microvascular complications 25% (p=0.0099). No significant macrovascular benefit at 10 years. Metformin subgroup: 39% MI reduction, 36% all-cause mortality reduction.
impact: Established microvascular benefit of glycemic control. Metformin preferred first-line in T2DM (UKPDS Metformin substudy). 10-year follow-up: legacy effect — sustained benefits even after randomization ended.

subtitle: Empagliflozin reduces CV death in T2DM + CVD
endpoint: 3-point MACE (CV death, MI, stroke)
finding: Empagliflozin reduced CV death 38% (3.7% vs 5.9%/yr, HR 0.62, p<0.001). HF hospitalization reduced 35%. Renal protection significant. No reduction in non-fatal MI or stroke.
impact: First SGLT2i CV outcomes trial. CV death benefit appeared within months → hemodynamic mechanism (not anti-atherosclerotic). Established SGLT2i for T2DM + CVD as standard of care. Pearl: cardiorenal benefits independent of glucose lowering.

subtitle: Liraglutide reduces MACE in T2DM + CVD
endpoint: 3-point MACE
finding: Liraglutide reduced MACE 13% (HR 0.87, p=0.01). CV death reduced 22%. All-cause mortality reduced 15%. HF neutral (unlike SGLT2i). Weight loss 2.3kg. Slowed renal progression.
impact: Established GLP-1 RA (liraglutide first, then semaglutide SUSTAIN-6) for T2DM + ASCVD. Primary benefit: MACE reduction (anti-atherosclerotic effect, slower onset than SGLT2i). Choose GLP-1 RA when ASCVD predominates; SGLT2i when HF or CKD predominates.

subtitle: Canagliflozin for kidney disease in T2DM
endpoint: Kidney failure, SCr doubling, CV/renal death
finding: Canagliflozin reduced primary composite 30% (HR 0.70, p<0.001). ESKD reduced 32%. HF hospitalization reduced 39%. Trial stopped early at interim for kidney endpoint benefit.
impact: First trial stopped early specifically for renal benefit. Established SGLT2i for CKD in T2DM. KDIGO 2022: SGLT2i recommended for T2DM + CKD with eGFR ≥20. Can continue drug through dialysis once initiated. Pearl: initial eGFR dip (hemodynamic) is expected and does not indicate harm.

subtitle: Dapagliflozin in CKD with/without diabetes
endpoint: >50% eGFR decline, ESKD, or renal/CV death
finding: Dapagliflozin reduced primary endpoint 39% (HR 0.61, p<0.001) across eGFR 25-75. Benefit consistent regardless of diabetes status (key novel finding). AKI events reduced 27%.
impact: Extended nephroprotection beyond diabetic CKD — IgA nephropathy, FSGS, and other proteinuric CKD benefit. KDIGO 2022: SGLT2i for ALL CKD if eGFR ≥20 + UACR ≥200. Initial eGFR decline expected (hemodynamic) — do not discontinue for this alone.

subtitle: Intensive BP control (<120) in T2DM
endpoint: Non-fatal MI, non-fatal stroke, CV death
finding: Intensive BP NOT superior for primary composite (1.87% vs 2.09%/yr, p=0.20). Stroke only significantly reduced (0.32% vs 0.53%/yr). More adverse events (electrolyte, syncope, elevated creatinine).
impact: SPRINT (2015) showed SBP <120 benefited non-DM high-risk patients (25% MACE reduction). ACCORD: DM patients do not clearly benefit from SBP <120. Current guidelines: SBP <130 for T2DM (ADA, ACC/AHA). Pearl: ACCORD + SPRINT together highlight differential effects by DM status.

subtitle: Intensive BP control in high-CV-risk non-DM patients
drug: SBP target <120 vs <140 mmHg (multiple antihypertensive classes)
endpoint: MI, ACS, stroke, HF, CV death
finding: Intensive control reduced primary composite 25% (HR 0.75, p<0.001). All-cause mortality reduced 27%. Stopped at 3.3 years for clear benefit. More AKI (2.5x) and syncope in intensive group.
impact: Drove 2017 ACC/AHA guidelines to define hypertension as ≥130/80 (was ≥140/90). Target SBP <130 for high-risk non-DM adults. Note: SPRINT used automated unattended office BP — corresponds to ~5-7 mmHg lower than standard clinic measurement. Adjust targets accordingly.

subtitle: Surrogate endpoints vs clinical outcomes — lessons learned
endpoint: CAST: arrhythmia suppression + mortality | ISIS-2: vascular mortality
finding: CAST: Class IC drugs suppressed PVCs (surrogate benefit) but INCREASED mortality 2.5x vs placebo (10.2% vs 4.5%). ISIS-2: Aspirin alone reduced 5-week vascular mortality 23%. Streptokinase alone 25%. Both together 42% reduction.
impact: CAST: seminal lesson — surrogate endpoint improvement does NOT equal clinical benefit. Class IC agents now contraindicated post-MI or structural heart disease. ISIS-2: aspirin cornerstone of ACS therapy. Additive benefit of aspirin + fibrinolytic.

subtitle: Apixaban for acute VTE treatment
endpoint: Recurrent VTE or VTE-related death; major bleeding
finding: Apixaban non-inferior for recurrent VTE (2.3% vs 2.7%) with 69% less major bleeding (0.6% vs 1.8%, p<0.001) vs enoxaparin/warfarin. No bridging needed.
impact: Established apixaban as preferred DOAC for VTE: same efficacy, far less bleeding. BID x7d then QD dosing. Now first-line in most guidelines.

subtitle: Rivaroxaban for acute DVT and PE
endpoint: Recurrent symptomatic VTE; major or CRNM bleeding
finding: Rivaroxaban non-inferior for recurrent VTE (2.1% vs 3.0% DVT; 2.1% vs 1.8% PE). No bridging required — single-drug treatment approach.
impact: First DOAC approved for VTE treatment. Introduced concept of single-agent oral anticoagulation (no heparin bridge). 15 mg BID x21d → 20 mg QD.

subtitle: Dabigatran for VTE treatment
endpoint: Recurrent VTE; major bleeding
finding: Dabigatran 150 mg BID non-inferior to warfarin for recurrent VTE (2.4% vs 2.1%) after initial heparin bridging ≥5 days. Similar major bleeding.
impact: Established dabigatran in VTE. Requires 5-10 day heparin lead-in (unlike apixaban/rivaroxaban). Renal dose adjustment critical (avoid CrCl <30).

subtitle: Edoxaban vs LMWH in cancer-associated VTE
endpoint: Composite of recurrent VTE or major bleeding at 12 months
finding: Edoxaban non-inferior to dalteparin (12.8% vs 13.5%). More major GI bleeding with edoxaban (3.7% vs 0.9%). Superiority on recurrent VTE (7.9% vs 11.3%).
impact: DOAC option for cancer VTE emerged alongside SELECT-D (rivaroxaban). Caution with luminal GI cancers due to bleeding. ISTH: prefer DOAC over LMWH in most cancer VTE.

subtitle: Idarucizumab for dabigatran reversal
endpoint: Maximum percentage reversal of dabigatran anticoagulant effect
finding: 100% reversal of dabigatran within minutes in >88% of patients. Unbound dabigatran unmeasurable in most. Median time to hemostasis 2.5h.
impact: Established idarucizumab as specific reversal agent for dabigatran. 5g IV given as two 2.5g doses. Now standard for life-threatening bleeding or urgent surgery.

subtitle: Andexanet alfa for factor Xa inhibitor reversal
endpoint: Percent change in anti-Xa activity; hemostatic efficacy at 12h
finding: Reduced anti-Xa activity 92% (apixaban) and 97% (rivaroxaban) within 2 minutes. Good/excellent hemostasis in 82% at 12 hours.
impact: Only approved specific reversal for apixaban and rivaroxaban. High dose for rivaroxaban >8h or apixaban; low dose otherwise. Thrombotic risk ~10% in first 30 days.

subtitle: Bridging anticoagulation in Afib perioperatively
endpoint: Arterial thromboembolism (ATE); major bleeding
finding: No significant difference in ATE (0.4% bridge vs 0.3% no-bridge). Significantly more major bleeding with bridging (3.2% vs 1.3%, p=0.005).
impact: Low-to-moderate stroke risk Afib patients do NOT benefit from bridging — bridging increases bleeding without reducing stroke. High-risk (mechanical valves, recent stroke) still bridge.

subtitle: ACCP/AHA VTE treatment and prophylaxis guidelines
endpoint: Grade 1A/2B recommendations for VTE management
finding: DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban) preferred over VKA for most VTE (Grade 2B). LMWH preferred in pregnancy. Extended therapy (>3 months) in unprovoked VTE. Prophylaxis thresholds defined.
impact: Core reference for VTE decisions on BCPS: duration of therapy (provoked 3 months, unprovoked 3-6+ months), choice of agent, and who needs extended therapy.

subtitle: Finerenone in diabetic CKD
endpoint: Kidney failure, eGFR decline ≥40%, renal death; CV composite
finding: Finerenone reduced primary kidney composite 18% (HR 0.82, 95% CI 0.73–0.93) and CV composite 14% vs placebo in T2DM CKD.
impact: Established finerenone (nonsteroidal MRA) as 5th pillar for diabetic CKD on top of ACEi/ARB, SGLT2i, GLP-1. Less hyperkalemia than spironolactone.

subtitle: Finerenone: CV outcomes in diabetic CKD
endpoint: CV death, non-fatal MI, non-fatal stroke, HF hospitalization
finding: Finerenone reduced CV composite 13% (HR 0.87, 95% CI 0.76–0.98) vs placebo. Consistent with FIDELIO-DKD (pooled FIDELITY analysis: 14% CV risk reduction).
impact: Together FIDELIO + FIGARO = FIDELITY: 13,026 patients confirm finerenone benefits in CV+kidney outcomes. Now included in ADA and KDIGO guidelines.

subtitle: Simvastatin/ezetimibe in CKD
endpoint: Major atherosclerotic events (MI, coronary revascularization, stroke)
finding: Simvastatin/ezetimibe reduced major atherosclerotic events 17% (11.3% vs 13.4%, RR 0.83, p=0.0021). Benefit in CKD3-5 and dialysis subgroups.
impact: Established lipid-lowering therapy in CKD. Statins alone less effective in dialysis patients. Simvastatin dose capped at 20 mg with ezetimibe. KDIGO recommends starting if CKD ≥50 years.

subtitle: Rosuvastatin/atorvastatin in dialysis patients
endpoint: CV death and non-fatal MI/stroke
finding: AURORA: rosuvastatin did NOT reduce CV events in hemodialysis (HR 0.96, p=0.59). 4D: atorvastatin did not significantly reduce primary endpoint in diabetic hemodialysis.
impact: Statins do NOT benefit patients already on chronic dialysis — likely too late in CV disease progression. Start statins BEFORE dialysis. KDIGO: do not initiate new statins in dialysis.

subtitle: Darbepoetin in T2DM and CKD anemia
endpoint: Death/CV event; transfusion rate; ESRD
finding: Darbepoetin targeting Hgb ~13 g/dL increased stroke risk (101 vs 53 events, HR 1.92, p<0.001) with no benefit on CV events or ESRD vs transfusion-triggered approach.
impact: Established AGAINST targeting normal hemoglobin with ESAs. Target Hgb 10–11.5 g/dL in CKD. KDIGO: avoid Hgb >11.5 in CKD not on dialysis. Correction: ESAs for sx anemia, Hgb <10.

subtitle: Cinacalcet for secondary hyperparathyroidism
endpoint: Composite of death, MI, unstable angina, HF, peripheral vascular disease
finding: No CV mortality benefit in EVOLVE. PTH lowering confirmed. Used for symptomatic hypercalcemia and uncontrolled PTH in dialysis patients.
impact: KDIGO: cinacalcet for persistent elevated PTH in dialysis; not for CV benefit alone. Monitor: hypocalcemia (hold if Ca <8.4 mg/dL), nausea/vomiting.

subtitle: KDIGO CKD management guidelines update
endpoint: eGFR-based staging, drug therapy thresholds
finding: Recommend SGLT2i (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) in all CKD eGFR ≥20 with or without T2DM. Finerenone in T2DM CKD + albuminuria despite max RAAS. ACEi/ARB first-line.
impact: Comprehensive staging (G1-G5, A1-A3), dosing cutoffs for major drug classes, referral triggers, and CKD-mineral bone disorder management. Core BCPS reference.

subtitle: Tiotropium in COPD — mortality and lung function
endpoint: Rate of FEV1 decline; exacerbations; mortality
finding: Tiotropium improved FEV1 (87 mL over 4 years), reduced exacerbations 14%, and reduced hospitalizations. Mortality reduction not significant but consistent trend.
impact: Established tiotropium as preferred maintenance bronchodilator in COPD. LAMA = first-line for GOLD B/E. Once-daily dosing. Reduce exacerbations and improve quality of life.

subtitle: Salmeterol/Fluticasone vs mono in COPD
endpoint: All-cause mortality; exacerbations; health status
finding: Combination ICS/LABA reduced exacerbations 25% vs placebo. Mortality benefit did not meet pre-specified significance (p=0.052). Increased pneumonia with ICS (19.6% vs 12.3%).
impact: Established ICS/LABA for severe COPD. ICS-associated pneumonia risk real and dose-dependent. Budesonide/formoterol associated with less pneumonia than fluticasone in some trials.

subtitle: Triple therapy (FF/UMEC/VI) vs dual in COPD
endpoint: Moderate-severe exacerbations; spirometry; mortality
finding: Triple therapy reduced exacerbations 15% vs LAMA/LABA and 25% vs ICS/LABA. Reduced all-cause mortality 42% vs LAMA/LABA (p=0.003). More pneumonia vs LAMA/LABA.
impact: Triple therapy superior to dual in high-risk COPD (≥2 exacerbations/yr or ≥1 hospitalization). Now GOLD E recommendation. ICS component for eosinophils >300 cells/μL most benefit.

subtitle: Mepolizumab for severe eosinophilic asthma
endpoint: Exacerbation rate; OCS dose reduction
finding: MENSA: mepolizumab reduced exacerbations 47-53% vs placebo. SIRIUS: reduced OCS dose by median 50% (vs 0% placebo). Blood eos ≥150 cells/μL at start or ≥300 in prior year.
impact: Established anti-IL-5 biologics for severe eosinophilic asthma. Threshold: blood eos ≥150 cells/μL. Monthly SQ injection. Other anti-IL-5s: reslizumab (IV), benralizumab (anti-IL-5Rα).

subtitle: Dupilumab for moderate-severe uncontrolled asthma
endpoint: Annualized severe exacerbation rate; FEV1; OCS use
finding: Dupilumab reduced exacerbations 48% (eos ≥300 cells/μL subgroup: 68%) and improved FEV1 by 210 mL. Eosinophilia transient. No significant benefit in eos <300.
impact: Dupilumab (anti-IL-4Rα = blocks IL-4 and IL-13) approved for moderate-severe uncontrolled asthma. Also approved for OCS-dependent asthma, atopic dermatitis, CRSwNP. Q2W SQ.

subtitle: Nintedanib in idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis
endpoint: Annual rate of FVC decline; acute exacerbations; survival
finding: Nintedanib reduced FVC decline 50% (−114.7 mL/yr vs −223.5 mL/yr, p<0.001). Time to first acute exacerbation improved in INPULSIS-2. Main AE: diarrhea 62%.
impact: One of two approved antifibrotic agents for IPF (other: pirfenidone). Reduces disease progression but does not cure IPF. Liver function monitoring required. Start early in course.

subtitle: GOLD COPD assessment and pharmacotherapy
endpoint: Symptom-based ABCD assessment + exacerbation history
finding: GOLD 2024: GOLD A → SABA; GOLD B → LAMA or LAMA/LABA; GOLD E (formerly C/D with exacerbations) → LAMA/LABA first-line, ICS add-on if eos ≥300 or on maximal LAMA/LABA.
impact: Foundation of COPD pharmacotherapy decisions on BCPS. GOLD B changed: ALL Grade B should start dual bronchodilator. ICS only in E group or eos >300. Step-up if uncontrolled.

subtitle: IDSA/ATS community-acquired pneumonia management
endpoint: Treatment recommendations by severity and site of care
finding: Outpatient: amoxicillin 1g TID (healthy) or combo beta-lactam + macrolide (comorbidities). Inpatient: beta-lactam + macrolide or respiratory FQ. ICU: beta-lactam + azithromycin + antipseudomonal if risk.
impact: Dropped requirement for blood cultures in non-ICU CAP. De-emphasized atypical coverage in outpatient low-risk. Duration: 5 days if clinical improvement (not 7-10). Core BCPS reference.

subtitle: Clostridioides difficile infection management
endpoint: Cure, recurrence prevention, severity classification
finding: Fidaxomicin preferred over vancomycin for initial nonsevere CDI (10-day course). Vancomycin acceptable if cost prohibits fidaxomicin. Bezlotoxumab for first recurrence prevention in high-risk. AVOID metronidazole for most CDI.
impact: Major shift: fidaxomicin is now preferred (less recurrence 15% vs 25%). Metronidazole no longer recommended even for mild CDI. Fecal microbiota transplant for recurrent CDI.

subtitle: Treatment of MRSA infections
endpoint: Treatment by infection type: bacteremia, skin/soft tissue, pneumonia, CNS
finding: Vancomycin AUC/MIC-guided dosing (400-600 mg·h/L) preferred over trough-based. Daptomycin alternative for bacteremia/endocarditis (NOT pneumonia - inactivated by surfactant). Linezolid for MRSA pneumonia.
impact: Vancomycin AUC dosing now standard (2020 update). Target AUC 400-600, not trough 15-20. Daptomycin 6mg/kg for bacteremia, 8-10mg/kg for endocarditis. Combination therapy for persistent bacteremia.

subtitle: International sepsis management guidelines
endpoint: Hour-1 and Hour-6 bundle; 30-day mortality
finding: 1-hour bundle: blood cultures, antibiotics within 1h of sepsis recognition, 30 mL/kg crystalloid if hypoperfusion, vasopressors if MAP <65. Norepinephrine first-line vasopressor. Hydrocortisone for refractory shock.
impact: Crystalloid resuscitation now targets individualized endpoints (lactate, CVP, ScvO2). Balanced solutions (LR) preferred over NS for reducing AKI. De-escalate antibiotics based on cultures.

subtitle: Long-acting lipoglycopeptides for skin infections
endpoint: Early clinical response (72h) for ABSSSI
finding: Single-dose oritavancin and 2-dose dalbavancin non-inferior to vancomycin for ABSSSI. Comparable cure rates (~80%). No renal monitoring needed.
impact: Long-acting lipoglycopeptides enable outpatient OPAT for serious skin infections. Single dose oritavancin (1200 mg) or 2-dose dalbavancin (1500 mg then 500 mg). Not active against VRE. Avoid with UFH.

subtitle: IDSA bone and joint infection guidelines
endpoint: Duration of therapy; IV-to-oral transition; surgical indications
finding: Native vertebral osteomyelitis: 6 weeks IV (or IV-to-oral step-down). Septic arthritis: 3-4 weeks total. IV-to-oral switch appropriate when clinical stability achieved and oral bioavailability ≥90% (fluoroquinolones, linezolid, TMP-SMX).
impact: Supports early IV-to-oral transition in stable osteomyelitis with high-bioavailability oral agents (OVIVA trial 2019 confirmed non-inferiority of oral therapy). Key concept for BCPS: PK/PD justification.

subtitle: Effectiveness of atypical antipsychotics in schizophrenia
endpoint: Time to discontinuation for any cause (proxy for effectiveness)
finding: Olanzapine had longest time to discontinuation (9.2 months vs 4-6 months for others). BUT highest rates of metabolic side effects. Perphenazine (FGA) comparable to most SGAs.
impact: Challenged superiority of second-generation antipsychotics. All SGAs have significant side effects. Olanzapine most effective but metabolic risk limits use. Individualize selection by side effect profile.

subtitle: Sequential treatment alternatives for depression
drug: Citalopram, bupropion, sertraline, venlafaxine, lithium augmentation
endpoint: Remission rate at each treatment step; relapse after remission
finding: Step 1 (citalopram): 28% remission. Step 2 switch/augment: 26-29% additional. Step 3: 14-20%. Step 4: 13%. Cumulative remission 67% but relapse high. Each step: adequate trial (8-12 weeks).
impact: Real-world evidence for treatment-resistant depression. Most patients do not achieve remission with first SSRI. Augmentation strategies (bupropion, lithium, thyroid) evidence-based. Try 2-3 agents before declaring TRD.

subtitle: Canadian Network for Mood and Anxiety Treatments bipolar guideline
endpoint: Acute mania, acute depression, maintenance, mixed features
finding: Acute mania 1st line: lithium, valproate, or AAP (quetiapine, aripiprazole, risperidone). Acute bipolar depression: quetiapine, lurasidone, lamotrigine. Maintenance: lithium best evidence. Avoid antidepressant monotherapy.
impact: Lithium remains gold standard for bipolar maintenance (suicide prevention, nephroprotective if early use). Monitor lithium levels (0.6-1.2 mEq/L), renal function, thyroid. Narrow TI drug.

subtitle: Suicidality risk with antidepressants in youth
endpoint: Suicidal ideation and behavior in children/adolescents
finding: 2x increased risk of suicidal thinking/behavior with antidepressants vs placebo in patients <18 years (4% vs 2%). No completed suicides. Risk present for all antidepressants.
impact: Black box warning for all antidepressants: close monitoring in <18 years (especially first 4 weeks), patients 18-24. Not a contraindication to use — untreated depression also high risk. Weekly visits initially.

subtitle: Clozapine REMS program for agranulocytosis monitoring
endpoint: Absolute neutrophil count (ANC) monitoring thresholds
finding: ANC ≥1500 cells/μL to initiate; ≥1000 to continue in general population (≥500 in benign ethnic neutropenia). Stop if ANC <1000 (severe neutropenia) or <500 (agranulocytosis). Monthly ANC after 6 months.
impact: Clozapine only antipsychotic with evidence for treatment-resistant schizophrenia and schizophrenia suicidality. REMS required for prescribing and dispensing. ANC monitoring mandatory. Seizure risk dose-dependent.

subtitle: Treatment of PTSD: pharmacotherapy and psychotherapy
endpoint: PTSD symptom reduction, hyperarousal, nightmares
finding: Trauma-focused psychotherapy (PE, CPT) STRONGLY recommended first. Pharmacotherapy: sertraline and paroxetine (FDA-approved for PTSD) as adjuncts. Prazosin for trauma-related nightmares.
impact: SSRIs are adjunct to psychotherapy in PTSD — not primary treatment alone. Prazosin (alpha-1 blocker): titrate to 15 mg QHS for nightmares. Avoid benzodiazepines (worsens outcomes).

subtitle: Infliximab + azathioprine vs mono in Crohns disease
endpoint: Corticosteroid-free clinical remission at week 26
finding: Combination therapy superior: 57% remission vs 44% (infliximab alone) vs 30% (AZA alone). Mucosal healing: 44% vs 30% vs 17% respectively.
impact: Established combination biologic + immunomodulator as gold standard for moderate-severe Crohn's. Risk: lymphoma with combination therapy (thiopurine + anti-TNF). Monitoring: TB, CBC, hepatic panel.

subtitle: Vedolizumab in UC and Crohns disease
endpoint: Clinical remission at week 6 and 52; endoscopic improvement
finding: GEMINI 1 (UC): vedolizumab achieved remission 17.3% vs 5.5% at week 6; 41.8% vs 15.9% at week 52. GEMINI 2 (CD): week 52 remission 39% vs 22%. Gut-selective mechanism.
impact: Vedolizumab: gut-selective anti-integrin (α4β7) — lower systemic immunosuppression, preferred in elderly and those with extra-intestinal infections. No TB reactivation risk unlike anti-TNFs.

subtitle: Direct-acting antivirals for hepatitis C
endpoint: Sustained virologic response at 12 weeks (SVR12)
finding: DAAs achieved >95% SVR12 without interferon. Genotype testing no longer required with pan-genotypic regimens. 8-12 weeks depending on genotype and cirrhosis status.
impact: DAAs revolutionized HCV treatment. Interferon-free 8-12 week regimens with minimal AEs. Screen ALL adults >18 (USPSTF). Drug interactions via CYP3A4, P-gp, transporters — crucial for BCPS.

subtitle: Management of decompensated cirrhosis
endpoint: SBP prevention, HRS management, hepatic encephalopathy, varices
finding: Albumin 1.5g/kg IV day 1 + 1g/kg IV day 3 for SBP: reduces HRS and mortality. Rifaximin + lactulose superior to lactulose alone for secondary prevention of HE. NSBBs (propranolol, carvedilol) for varices prophylaxis.
impact: Key pharmacotherapy decisions: ceftriaxone for SBP, norfloxacin for primary SBP prophylaxis (ascites + low-protein ascitic fluid), albumin for large volume paracentesis (6-8g/L removed).

subtitle: ACG/AGA H. pylori treatment guidelines
drug: PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin; bismuth quadruple; vonoprazan-based
endpoint: Eradication rates by regimen and resistance pattern
finding: Clarithromycin resistance >15%: use bismuth quadruple (PPI + bismuth + metronidazole + tetracycline) x14 days (>90% eradication). Clarithromycin triple therapy only if local resistance <15%.
impact: Clarithromycin-based triple therapy no longer empirically recommended in most US areas (>20% resistance). Vonoprazan-based regimens emerging (FDA 2022). Test-and-treat if ≥60 years or alarm symptoms.

subtitle: Pembrolizumab vs chemotherapy in high PD-L1 NSCLC
endpoint: PFS; OS; ORR in PD-L1 TPS ≥50% NSCLC
finding: Pembrolizumab reduced disease progression 50% vs chemo (PFS 10.3 vs 6.0 months, HR 0.50). OS: 30 vs 14.2 months. ORR 45% vs 28%. Grade 3-5 toxicity 26% vs 53%.
impact: Established PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor as first-line in high PD-L1 (≥50%) NSCLC without driver mutations. PD-L1 testing mandatory. irAEs: pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis — manage with corticosteroids.

subtitle: Pertuzumab + trastuzumab in HER2+ metastatic breast cancer
endpoint: PFS; OS; ORR in HER2+ mBC
finding: Addition of pertuzumab improved median OS 15.7 months (56.5 vs 40.8 months, HR 0.68, p<0.001). PFS improved 6.1 months. LVEF decline similar between arms.
impact: Dual HER2 blockade (pertuzumab + trastuzumab) is standard 1st-line in HER2+ metastatic BC. Pertuzumab blocks HER2-HER3 dimerization (different epitope from trastuzumab). Monitor LVEF every 3 months.

subtitle: Obinutuzumab and ibrutinib in CLL
endpoint: PFS; OS; CR rates
finding: CLL11: Obinutuzumab + chlorambucil superior to rituximab + chlorambucil in PFS (26.7 vs 15.2 months). RESONATE: Ibrutinib superior to ofatumumab in PFS (NR vs 8.1 months, HR 0.22).
impact: BTK inhibitors (ibrutinib, acalabrutinib, zanubrutinib) now front-line or preferred in CLL (especially del17p, TP53 mutation). Monitor: atrial fibrillation (ibrutinib ~16%), bleeding, hypertension. Hold before/after surgery.

subtitle: Prevention of chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting
endpoint: Complete response (no emesis, no rescue antiemetic)
finding: HEC regimens: triple therapy (5-HT3 + NK1 antagonist + dex) or quadruple (add olanzapine 10 mg). MEC: 5-HT3 + dex ± NK1 depending on emetogenicity. Olanzapine superior for delayed CINV.
impact: Olanzapine 10 mg QHS x4 days now recommended in HEC (reduces acute and delayed nausea). NK1 antagonists (aprepitant, fosaprepitant, netupitant-palonosetron): CYP3A4 interactions. Dexamethasone dose adjusted if aprepitant used.

subtitle: Belatacept vs cyclosporine in kidney transplant
endpoint: Patient/graft survival; eGFR at 3 years; acute rejection
finding: Belatacept: superior eGFR at 3 years (65 vs 50 mL/min/1.73m2, p<0.001). Similar patient/graft survival. Higher acute rejection rate in year 1 (22% vs 7%). Fewer metabolic complications.
impact: Belatacept (CTLA4-Ig, co-stimulation blocker) preserves kidney function better than CNI long-term. EBV-seronegative recipients: higher PTLD risk (contraindication). Monthly IV infusion.

subtitle: Consensus on tacrolimus therapeutic drug monitoring
endpoint: Trough levels (C0) and AUC targets by organ and time post-transplant
finding: Kidney Tx: C0 target 8-12 ng/mL (months 1-3), then 5-8 ng/mL (>6 months). Liver Tx: 8-12 ng/mL initially. CYP3A4/P-gp interactions major (azole antifungals, macrolides, rifampin, calcium channel blockers).
impact: Tacrolimus narrow TI: CYP3A4 inhibitors (azoles, verapamil) drastically increase levels; inducers (rifampin, St. John's Wort) decrease. Toxicities: nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity, PTDM, hypertension. AUC-based dosing emerging.

subtitle: Mycophenolate mofetil for rejection prophylaxis
endpoint: Treatment failure (biopsy-proven acute rejection, graft loss, death) at 6 months
finding: MMF 2g/day + cyclosporine: reduced biopsy-proven acute rejection 17-20% vs azathioprine (38% vs 17-20%). MMF preferred over azathioprine in all transplant types.
impact: MMF (inosine monophosphate dehydrogenase inhibitor) became standard anti-proliferative in transplant. Dose reduction for GI side effects: divide doses, use EC-MPS (Myfortic). Monitor CBC (leukopenia). Teratogenic — contraception required.

subtitle: AGS Beers Criteria for potentially inappropriate medications in older adults
endpoint: Medications to avoid or use with caution in ≥65 years
finding: Avoid: first-generation antihistamines (diphenhydramine), TCAs, benzodiazepines, nonbenzodiazepine hypnotics (Z-drugs), anticholinergic drugs, meperidine, PPIs (>8 weeks), sliding-scale insulin. With caution: SNRIs, anticonvulsants.
impact: Anticholinergic burden linked to delirium, falls, cognitive decline. Benzodiazepines: 2-3x fall risk. NSAIDs: GI/renal/CV risk. Alternative: acetaminophen for pain. Deprescribing as important as prescribing.

subtitle: Antihypertensive treatment in very elderly (≥80 years)
endpoint: Fatal stroke; all-cause mortality; cardiovascular events
finding: Indapamide ± perindopril reduced fatal stroke 39%, all-cause mortality 21%, CV events 34% vs placebo in patients ≥80 years. Terminated early for benefit. Target BP <150/80 mmHg.
impact: Established antihypertensive therapy in very elderly. JNC8/ACC/AHA: target <130/80 in fit elderly; <150 systolic acceptable in frail. Caution with orthostatic hypotension (fall risk). Start low, go slow.

subtitle: European consensus on prescribing in older adults
endpoint: Potentially inappropriate prescriptions (PIPs) and prescribing omissions (POs)
finding: STOPP (Screening Tool of Older Persons Prescriptions): 143 criteria for potentially inappropriate prescribing. START (Screening Tool to Alert to Right Treatment): prescribing omissions that should be considered.
impact: Complements Beers Criteria (US vs European perspective). Key additions: proton pump inhibitors at high doses >8 weeks, NSAIDs in CKD stages 3-5, opioids without laxatives. Use both tools together for medication review.

subtitle: Celecoxib vs ibuprofen vs naproxen CV safety
endpoint: MACE (CV death, non-fatal MI, non-fatal stroke)
finding: Celecoxib 100mg BID non-inferior to ibuprofen 600mg TID and naproxen 375mg BID for CV events (2.3% vs 2.7% vs 2.5%, p<0.05 for non-inferiority). Lower GI events and renal events with celecoxib.
impact: Challenged notion that all COX-2 inhibitors are more cardiotoxic than non-selective NSAIDs. At anti-inflammatory doses, celecoxib has similar CV risk. All NSAIDs increase CV risk vs placebo. Celecoxib preferable for GI safety.

subtitle: CDC Clinical Practice Guideline for opioid prescribing
endpoint: Appropriate use, dosing, monitoring, and tapering of opioids for chronic pain
finding: Non-opioid therapies preferred for chronic pain (SNRIs, TCAs, gabapentinoids, topicals, PT, CBT). If opioid needed: lowest effective dose, immediate-release first. MME thresholds: caution ≥50 MME/day; avoid >90 MME/day without specialist.
impact: Co-prescribe naloxone for ≥50 MME/day or concurrent benzodiazepine use. Buprenorphine preferred for chronic pain with opioid use disorder. Taper no faster than 10% per week. Avoid abrupt discontinuation.

subtitle: Treatment of opioid use disorder with buprenorphine
endpoint: Reduction in illicit opioid use; retention in treatment; overdose mortality
finding: Buprenorphine/naloxone reduces opioid use and mortality 50-75%. Methadone equivalent efficacy. Start buprenorphine in mild-moderate withdrawal (COWS ≥8-12). No upper dose limit established if tolerated.
impact: MOUD (medication for OUD) dramatically reduces overdose deaths. X-waiver eliminated in US (2023) — any DEA-licensed provider can prescribe buprenorphine. Naloxone add-on prevents IV misuse. Precipitated withdrawal risk if used too early.

subtitle: ASHP/IDSA/SIDP vancomycin monitoring guidelines
endpoint: Optimal PK/PD target: AUC/MIC; nephrotoxicity reduction
finding: AUC/MIC 400-600 mg·h/L (assuming MIC ≤1) optimal for efficacy and safety. Trough-based monitoring alone abandoned (troughs 15-20 caused nephrotoxicity without AUC benefit). Bayesian dosing preferred.
impact: Major practice change: use AUC-guided not trough-guided vancomycin dosing. First-dose AUC estimation via 2-level Bayesian software. Initial dose: 15-20 mg/kg q8-12h based on CrCl. AKI risk significantly lower with AUC vs trough approach.

subtitle: Extended-interval aminoglycoside dosing evidence
endpoint: Efficacy (Cmax/MIC ≥8-10); nephrotoxicity rates; ototoxicity
finding: Once-daily aminoglycoside dosing (ODA): Cmax/MIC ≥8-10 (concentration-dependent killing) with adequate PAE. Comparable or superior efficacy vs traditional dosing; similar or lower nephrotoxicity in ODA. Monitoring: 6-12h random level on Hartford nomogram.
impact: Extended-interval dosing (gentamicin 5-7 mg/kg q24h or ODA) preferred for most gram-negative infections. Exception: endocarditis (still uses synergistic dosing). Monitor: nephrotoxicity (BUN/SCr) and ototoxicity (high-frequency hearing loss).

subtitle: Continuous vs extended infusion beta-lactams for resistant organisms
endpoint: T>MIC ≥40-70% (bacteriostatic) or ≥100% (bactericidal) of dosing interval
finding: Extended infusion beta-lactams improve PTA (probability of target attainment) for resistant organisms without increasing total drug exposure or toxicity.
impact: Extended infusion pip/tazo, cefepime, meropenem recommended for MDR organisms and ICU patients. Stability limits: pip/tazo reconstituted in NS (not D5W) stable 12h at room temp. Practical implication for BCPS: PD rationale T>MIC.

subtitle: Clinical significance of CYP enzyme interactions
design: FDA drug interaction guidance + clinical studies
endpoint: Fold-change in AUC; clinical significance classification
finding: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, clarithromycin, ritonavir) increase substrate AUC ≥5-fold. Strong inducers (rifampin, carbamazepine) decrease ≥80%. Fluoxetine/paroxetine: strong CYP2D6 inhibitors (increase TCA, codeine, tamoxifen levels).
impact: BCPS must know: CYP3A4 inhibitors increase tacrolimus, apixaban, statins. CYP3A4 inducers decrease warfarin, hormonal contraceptives. P-gp interactions: digoxin (rifampin ↓digoxin 30%). Time course: enzyme induction takes 7-14 days; inhibition immediate.

subtitle: Reporting standards for randomized controlled trials
design: Expert consensus + systematic review of RCT reporting
endpoint: Checklist for transparent reporting of RCTs
finding: 25-item checklist for reporting RCTs: title/abstract, intro, methods (randomization, blinding, allocation concealment), results (participant flow, baseline, outcomes), discussion.
impact: CONSORT: gold standard for evaluating RCT reporting quality. Look for: proper randomization, concealment, ITT analysis, blinding. Absent concealment → overestimation of treatment effect (up to 40%). Key BCPS biostatistics concept.

subtitle: Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-analyses
endpoint: Heterogeneity assessment; publication bias; forest plots
finding: PRISMA 2020: 27-item checklist for systematic reviews. Heterogeneity (I2): <25% low, 25-75% moderate, >75% high. Fixed-effects vs random-effects models. Funnel plot asymmetry = publication bias.
impact: BCPS literature evaluation: understand I2 (can you pool?), forest plots (point estimates + CI), and publication bias (Egger test, funnel plot). Random-effects model preferred when I2 >25%. GRADE system for evidence quality.

subtitle: FDA guidance on adaptive clinical trial designs
endpoint: Enrichment designs; sample size re-estimation; seamless phase 2/3
finding: Adaptive designs allow preplanned modifications (sample size, endpoint, population) based on interim analyses without inflating Type I error. Bayesian vs frequentist approaches. Pragmatic trials: real-world effectiveness (vs explanatory/efficacy).
impact: Modern BCPS question: recognize study designs. Platform trials (RECOVERY, SOLIDARITY for COVID). Basket (tumor-agnostic), umbrella (multiple arms by biomarker), adaptive enrichment. Understand ITT vs PP (per-protocol) analysis differences.
